_meta.authoringNotes: Outbreak figures and case counts from WHO Ebola virus disease fact sheet 2024 and outbreak situation reports through end-2023 (West Africa 2014-2016: 28,616 cases / 11,310 deaths; DRC 10th outbreak 2018-2020: 3,470 / 2,287; Uganda Sudan 2022). Vaccine data per Henao-Restrepo et al. Lancet 2017 (rVSV-ZEBOV ring-vaccination trial, PMID 29362107) and Pollard et al. Lancet Infect Dis 2021 (Zabdeno/Mvabea two-dose). Monoclonal antibody efficacy per Mulangu et al. NEJM 2019 PALM trial (PMID 31774950): mortality 33% with Inmazeb (REGN-EB3) and 35% with Ebanga (ansuvimab) versus 51% with ZMapp. Pathophysiology per Feldmann & Geisbert Lancet 2011 (PMID 21084112), Jacob et al. Nat Rev Dis Primers 2020 (PMID 32080475). Survivor sequelae per Vetter et al. Lancet Infect Dis 2016 (PMID 26654122). Quarantine and biosafety per CDC Ebola guidelines 2023 and WHO clinical management interim guidelines 2022.

h1Title: Ebola Virus Disease: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Ebola virus disease is a severe and often fatal filoviral illness transmitted through direct contact with the blood or other body fluids of infected people, primates, or fruit bats, with case-fatality rates of 25-90% across outbreaks and a median around 50%. The 2014-2016 West African outbreak — the largest in recorded history — caused 28,616 confirmed and probable cases and 11,310 deaths across Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone; the 10th Democratic Republic of Congo outbreak in 2018-2020 caused 3,470 cases and 2,287 deaths; and the 2022 Sudan-strain outbreak in Uganda killed 55 of 142 confirmed cases. Six Ebola virus species are recognized, of which Zaire ebolavirus is the most lethal and the target of approved vaccines and antibody treatments. Patients develop fever, severe headache and myalgia, then gastrointestinal symptoms with profuse vomiting and diarrhoea, and in many a hemorrhagic phase leading to shock and multi-organ failure within 6-16 days. Two monoclonal antibody products (Inmazeb and Ebanga) approved in 2020 reduce mortality of Zaire ebolavirus infection from over 50% to under 35% when started early, and the rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo) vaccine is over 97% effective against Zaire infection. Supportive care — aggressive intravenous fluids, electrolyte replacement, and organ support — remains the foundation of treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Cumulative recorded cases since first identification in 1976 exceed 35,000, with more than 15,000 deaths across more than 30 outbreaks (WHO 2024)
keyStats.demographics: All ages affected; healthcare workers, family caregivers, and burial-rite participants carry the highest occupational risk
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at infection 25-35 years across major outbreaks
keyStats.globalCases: More than 35,000 cumulative recorded cases since 1976; case-fatality rate 25-90% across outbreaks (median ~50%); Zaire ebolavirus strain has the highest CFR

definition: Ebola virus disease (ICD-10: A98.4) is a severe, often fatal viral hemorrhagic fever caused by infection with one of six recognized Ebola virus species in the family Filoviridae: Zaire ebolavirus (most virulent, responsible for the largest outbreaks and the target of approved vaccines and antibody treatments), Sudan ebolavirus (case-fatality 40-60% across outbreaks), Bundibugyo ebolavirus (case-fatality 25-40%), Taï Forest ebolavirus (a single recorded human case), Reston ebolavirus (asymptomatic in humans), and Bombali ebolavirus (recently identified, no confirmed human disease). Ebola virus is a single-stranded negative-sense RNA filovirus that infects monocytes, macrophages, and dendritic cells, then spreads systemically to liver, spleen, adrenal glands, and endothelium, causing massive cytokine release, coagulopathy, vascular leakage, and multi-organ failure. The natural reservoir is the African fruit bat (Pteropodidae family); primary human infection follows contact with infected bats or non-human primates, after which the virus spreads person-to-person through direct contact with blood, vomitus, stool, sweat, breast milk, semen, saliva, and contaminated surfaces. Outbreaks occur predominantly in equatorial sub-Saharan Africa, with sporadic cases imported by infected travellers. Ebola virus disease is managed by infectious disease and emergency medicine teams within designated Ebola treatment units, with strict biosafety level 4 laboratory handling and integrated public-health response coordinated by ministries of health, WHO, and partners.

typesClassification.description: First identified in 1976 in Yambuku, Zaire (now DRC). Most lethal species with case-fatality rates of 60-90% historically and 30-65% in recent outbreaks with modern care. Caused the 2014-2016 West Africa outbreak and the 2018-2020 DRC outbreak. Approved vaccine (Ervebo, rVSV-ZEBOV) and antibody treatments (Inmazeb, Ebanga) target this species.

First identified in 1976 in Sudan. Case-fatality 40-60% across outbreaks. Caused the 2000-2001 Uganda outbreak and the 2022 Uganda outbreak (142 cases, 55 deaths). No species-specific licensed vaccine or antibody, although candidate vaccines are advancing.
Identified in 2007 in western Uganda. Case-fatality 25-40%. Caused outbreaks in Uganda (2007, 2012) and DRC (2012). Closer antigenic relationship to Zaire and Sudan viruses than to Taï Forest.
Single recorded human case in 1994 in a Swiss ethologist examining chimpanzees in Ivory Coast. Patient survived. No further human cases recorded.

Identified in 1989 in cynomolgus monkeys imported to Reston, Virginia. Causes lethal disease in non-human primates but has produced only asymptomatic seroconversion in humans. Circulates in pigs and primates in the Philippines.
Identified in 2018 in insectivorous bats in Sierra Leone. No confirmed human cases; pathogenicity in humans unknown.

primarySymptoms: Sudden onset of fever above 38 °C, severe frontal headache, profound fatigue, and diffuse muscle and joint pain 2-21 days (average 8-10 days) after exposure.
Sore throat with painful swallowing, chest pain, dry cough, and conjunctival injection in the first 3-5 days.
Profuse watery diarrhoea, recurrent vomiting, severe epigastric and right-upper-quadrant abdominal pain, and progressive dehydration starting day 3-7.
Maculopapular rash on the trunk, back, and limbs around day 5-7 — present in roughly 25-50% of patients and easily missed on dark skin.
Hemorrhagic features in roughly 30-50% of severe cases: bleeding from injection sites, gums, nose, gastrointestinal tract, vagina, and conjunctivae; petechiae; and ecchymoses.
Confusion, agitation, lethargy progressing to coma in late disease, reflecting both metabolic disturbance and direct central nervous system involvement.
Hiccups in late disease — a frequently described, poorly understood feature that often precedes death.
Shock with rapid thready pulse, cold extremities, low urine output, and severe metabolic acidosis as multi-organ failure develops on days 7-12.
Marked transaminase rise, often with AST greater than ALT, reflecting hepatic involvement.
Persistent virus in immune-privileged sites (semen, eye, central nervous system, breast milk) in survivors for months after clinical recovery.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden fever, severe headache, and myalgia in a person who has travelled from an active outbreak zone within the past 21 days
Fever and gastrointestinal symptoms in a healthcare worker who has had unprotected contact with an Ebola patient
Unexplained bleeding (gum bleeding, easy bruising, petechiae) in a returning traveller from an endemic region with fever
Severe conjunctival injection out of proportion to mild upper-respiratory symptoms
Profound fatigue and prostration disproportionate to apparent febrile illness in someone from an endemic zone
emergencySigns: Hypotension, tachycardia, cold extremities, and oliguria — septic-pattern shock; requires aggressive intravenous fluids and electrolytes within a designated treatment facility
Active hemorrhage from multiple sites with prolonged prothrombin time and falling platelets — disseminated intravascular coagulation
Persistent vomiting or diarrhoea producing >5 L of stool per day with severe dehydration and hypokalemia
Encephalopathy, seizures, or coma — central nervous system involvement or severe metabolic disturbance
Respiratory failure with hypoxia requiring supplemental oxygen — pulmonary involvement or volume overload
Pregnancy with confirmed or suspected Ebola — maternal and fetal mortality near 100% historically; requires expert care

causes.cause: Zoonotic spillover from infected bats or non-human primates
causes.description: Pteropodid fruit bats are the suspected natural reservoir. Index cases of outbreaks typically arise from handling, butchering, or eating infected bats, chimpanzees, gorillas, antelope, or duikers. Spillover events are rare but consistently the entry point of human outbreaks.

causes.cause: Direct human-to-human contact with infected body fluids
causes.description: Once introduced into humans, Ebola spreads through direct contact with blood, vomitus, stool, saliva, sweat, breast milk, semen, urine, tears, and contaminated surfaces. Skin and mucous membranes are entry points; intact skin can also be sufficient for transmission. Highest risk during late disease and after death.

Healthcare workers and caregivers face elevated risk from needlesticks, splashes, and contact with patient fluids. In the 2014-2016 outbreak, over 800 healthcare workers were infected and over 500 died. Strict PPE protocols and dedicated Ebola treatment units have substantially reduced this risk.

Direct contact with the body of a deceased person during washing, dressing, and traditional burial ceremonies has driven many transmission chains. Safe and dignified burial protocols designed in collaboration with communities are now standard outbreak response.

causes.cause: Sexual transmission and persistence in semen
causes.description: Ebola virus persists in semen of male survivors for months — viable virus has been detected up to 9-12 months and viral RNA beyond 2 years. Documented sexual transmission has triggered new outbreak clusters, and WHO recommends abstinence or condom use until semen has tested negative twice.

Pregnant women with Ebola infection have very high maternal and fetal mortality and the virus is transmitted through breast milk. Survivors who become pregnant or breastfeed can transmit the virus from persistently infected tissue.

riskFactors.factor: Residence or recent travel in an outbreak-affected country
riskFactors.description: Outbreaks have occurred in Guinea, Liberia, Sierra Leone, Democratic Republic of Congo, Uganda, Sudan, Republic of Congo, Gabon, Côte d'Ivoire, Mali, and Nigeria. Travelers without contact with sick people or wildlife face low individual risk but remain part of public-health surveillance during outbreaks.

Healthcare workers, ambulance staff, laboratory staff, and traditional healers caring for Ebola patients face the highest occupational risk. Risk is dramatically reduced by full PPE, designated treatment units, and trained donning/doffing teams.

Direct caregiving for a sick relative without PPE and traditional burial practices (washing and dressing the body) drive transmission within families and communities. Safe and dignified burial protocols substantially reduce risk.
Hunting, butchering, or consuming bushmeat is the route of zoonotic spillover that starts outbreaks. WHO recommends avoiding bushmeat consumption in endemic regions, particularly during outbreaks.

riskFactors.factor: Sexual contact with male Ebola survivors
riskFactors.description: Ebola virus persists in semen for months. WHO advises condom use until semen has tested negative on two consecutive monthly RT-PCR tests. New outbreak clusters have been documented years after survivors were declared clinically free of disease.

Pregnant women infected with Ebola have very high maternal mortality and almost universally lose the fetus. Pregnancy is also a documented driver of persistent viral shedding in survivors via amniotic and other fluids.

affectedDemographics: Geographic distribution: outbreaks limited almost exclusively to sub-Saharan Africa, with importation events to Spain, Italy, UK, US, Germany, and Norway during the 2014-2016 West African outbreak.
The largest outbreak (2014-2016 West Africa) recorded 28,616 confirmed and probable cases with 11,310 deaths (WHO).
Healthcare workers represented over 3% of all cases in 2014-2016, far above their share of population, reflecting occupational risk.
Women have higher participation in caregiving and burial rites and have shown slightly higher infection rates in some outbreaks; outcomes are similar by sex once infected.
Children account for roughly 20% of cases in major outbreaks; outcomes in infants and neonates are particularly poor.
diagnosisOverview: Clinical diagnosis is suspected in any patient with fever and compatible symptoms who has had potential exposure within 21 days (the maximum incubation period): travel from an active outbreak zone, contact with a known case, healthcare or laboratory work with Ebola samples, contact with bushmeat or wildlife, or unprotected sexual contact with a male survivor. Laboratory confirmation is by real-time reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) on blood, performed in a designated biosafety level 3 or 4 facility or with WHO-approved field cartridge platforms (such as GeneXpert). Detection of viral antigen by ELISA and IgM/IgG serology confirm exposure and recovery. Diagnostic yield rises after symptom day 3 — early in illness, viral load may be below detection thresholds and a negative test must be repeated 48-72 hours later if clinical suspicion remains high. Differential diagnosis is broad and includes other viral hemorrhagic fevers (Marburg, Lassa, Crimean-Congo, yellow fever), malaria (which must be ruled out and treated empirically while awaiting Ebola results), typhoid, shigellosis, leptospirosis, meningococcaemia, and severe sepsis from any cause. All suspected cases are isolated immediately in a designated Ebola treatment unit with strict PPE protocols. Imaging and ancillary investigations focus on assessing the severity of dehydration, organ injury, and coagulopathy: complete blood count typically shows leukopenia early, thrombocytopenia, and lymphopenia, with transaminitis (AST > ALT), elevated creatinine, and prolonged prothrombin time.

diagnosticTests.test: Real-time RT-PCR for Ebola virus RNA
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms acute Ebola virus infection — diagnostic standard during outbreaks
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample collected in a sealed tube within strict PPE protocol and transported to a designated laboratory; cartridge-based platforms (such as GeneXpert Ebola) can return results within 90-120 minutes at the bedside. Sensitivity rises after symptom day 3.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects circulating Ebola antigen; useful when PCR not available and in field laboratories
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample tested at a reference laboratory; results typically within 6-12 hours. Sensitivity lower than PCR.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies recent (IgM) or past (IgG) infection; supports diagnosis in late-presenting cases or for survivor follow-up
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample, often processed at a reference lab. IgM positive from day 4-10 and IgG positive from day 10 onwards.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and coagulation profile
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses haematological consequences — leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT/PTT
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard bedside or near-patient laboratory testing as part of routine treatment-unit monitoring; results within an hour.

diagnosticTests.test: Comprehensive metabolic panel (electrolytes, renal and liver function)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Monitors fluid resuscitation, renal injury, hepatic involvement, and electrolyte derangement
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same blood draw as the FBC; potassium, magnesium, calcium, AST, ALT, urea, and creatinine are followed daily during acute illness.

diagnosticTests.test: Malaria rapid diagnostic test and blood film
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out and/or treats coexisting malaria, which is common in endemic regions and shares early symptoms
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A drop of blood applied to a rapid antigen test cassette; result in 15-20 minutes. Microscopy adds quantitation if positive.

treatmentOverview: Treatment combines aggressive supportive care delivered in a dedicated Ebola treatment unit with disease-specific antibody therapy and rapid public-health containment. Supportive care is the foundation: aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation (often 5-10 L per day in severely dehydrated patients), correction of hypokalemia and hypocalcemia, treatment of nausea and pain, empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics for suspected bacterial coinfection, malaria treatment as appropriate, oxygen, blood-product transfusion for severe coagulopathy, and renal replacement therapy when needed. For Zaire ebolavirus infection, two monoclonal antibody products are now standard of care: Inmazeb (REGN-EB3, a cocktail of atoltivimab, maftivimab, and odesivimab) and Ebanga (ansuvimab-zykl). Both were approved by the US FDA in 2020 after the PALM trial in DRC demonstrated mortality of 33% (Inmazeb) and 35% (Ebanga) versus 51% with ZMapp and the historical baseline above 50%; benefit is greatest when treatment is given early, while viral load is still low. Patients with PCR cycle threshold above 22 (lower viral load) had mortality under 20% with either antibody. Supportive care and antibody treatment do not work in isolation from the public-health response: contact tracing, ring vaccination with rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo) of contacts and contacts of contacts, safe burial, community engagement, infection prevention and control in healthcare settings, and laboratory surveillance all contain transmission. For Sudan and Bundibugyo strains, no approved species-specific antibody or vaccine exists in 2026, although candidate vaccines and monoclonal antibodies are advancing in clinical trials. Survivor care includes screening for post-Ebola syndrome (arthralgia, uveitis, headache, depression, persistent virus in semen) and structured follow-up for at least one year.

medicalTreatments.name: Inmazeb (atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab, intravenous, single dose 50 mg/kg each component)
medicalTreatments.description: Three-monoclonal-antibody cocktail targeting the Ebola glycoprotein; FDA approved 2020. Neutralizes virus and reduces viral load rapidly. Given as a single 1- to 2-hour intravenous infusion as soon as Zaire ebolavirus infection is confirmed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mortality reduced from 51% (ZMapp) to 33% in PALM trial (Mulangu NEJM 2019, PMID 31774950); under 20% when viral load is low at treatment start

medicalTreatments.name: Ebanga (ansuvimab-zykl, intravenous, single dose 50 mg/kg)
medicalTreatments.description: Single-monoclonal-antibody product targeting the Ebola glycoprotein; FDA approved 2020. Given as a single intravenous infusion. Logistically simpler than Inmazeb and similar efficacy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mortality reduced to 35% in PALM trial (Mulangu NEJM 2019, PMID 31774950); similar efficacy advantage when given early

medicalTreatments.name: Aggressive intravenous fluid and electrolyte resuscitation
medicalTreatments.description: Severe gastrointestinal losses produce profound dehydration, hypokalemia, hypocalcemia, and acidosis. Patients may need 5-10 L of crystalloid per day with hourly clinical reassessment. Mortality drops substantially with structured fluid protocols.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mortality fell by 11-26 percentage points in cohorts with structured fluid and electrolyte protocols (Lamontagne et al. multicentre series)

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum empirical antibiotics
medicalTreatments.description: Empirical ceftriaxone or similar covers gut translocation bacteraemia and unrecognized typhoid or other coinfections. Standard of care during acute illness.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces secondary bacterial mortality but does not change Ebola viral course

medicalTreatments.name: Antimalarial therapy (artemether-lumefantrine or IV artesunate)
medicalTreatments.description: Empirical or treatment-confirmed malaria therapy is routine in endemic regions because malaria coinfection is common and clinically indistinguishable in early Ebola.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Treats coinfection; survivor outcomes improve when malaria is recognized and treated

medicalTreatments.name: Symptomatic care (antiemetics, analgesia, oxygen, nutrition)
medicalTreatments.description: Ondansetron and metoclopramide for vomiting, paracetamol-based analgesia (avoid NSAIDs because of bleeding risk), supplemental oxygen for hypoxia, and high-calorie nutritional support shorten time to recovery and reduce complications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement; reduced ICU length of stay in supported cohorts

surgicalOptions.name: Renal replacement therapy (continuous or intermittent hemodialysis)
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated for severe acute kidney injury, refractory hyperkalemia, or fluid overload in critically ill patients. Requires specially trained staff and biosafety-compliant equipment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Improves survival in selected patients with isolated renal failure when delivered in advanced units; case-series mortality benefit roughly 20-30%

alternativeTreatments.name: Convalescent plasma from Ebola survivors
alternativeTreatments.description: Historical option used during early outbreaks and the 2014-2016 epidemic. Phase 2/3 trials (Ebola-Tx, van Griensven NEJM 2016) showed no clear survival benefit. Replaced by approved monoclonal antibodies.

alternativeTreatments.name: Investigational antiviral therapy (remdesivir, favipiravir)
alternativeTreatments.description: Remdesivir and favipiravir were used in compassionate-access settings during the 2014-2016 and 2018-2020 outbreaks. Remdesivir was inferior to monoclonal antibodies in the PALM trial. Roles outside trials are limited.

whySeeSpecialist: Suspected Ebola requires immediate isolation in a designated treatment unit staffed by infectious disease, emergency, and critical care teams trained in biosafety protocols. Outside outbreak zones, suspected imported cases are coordinated by national public-health authorities, with reference laboratories handling all sample testing. Antibody treatment must be available within 24-48 hours of confirmation.
doctorSelectionGuide: In an outbreak setting, care is delivered through designated Ebola treatment units coordinated by the ministry of health, WHO, MSF, and partners. In non-endemic regions, suspected cases are referred to a national high-consequence infectious disease unit (CDC-designated regional Ebola treatment centre in the US; high-consequence infectious diseases network in the UK). Patients should not be transported to non-designated facilities once Ebola is suspected.
hospitalCriteria: Designated Ebola treatment unit with negative-pressure isolation
Trained donning/doffing teams using full PPE per WHO/CDC protocols
On-site or rapid-access RT-PCR with cartridge platform (e.g., GeneXpert Ebola)
Critical-care capacity for fluid resuscitation, renal support, mechanical ventilation
Pharmacy stocking approved monoclonal antibody therapy for Zaire ebolavirus
keyFacilities: WHO-designated Ebola treatment centres in active outbreak countries
High-consequence infectious disease units in the US, UK, and EU
BSL-4 reference laboratories (CDC, WHO Collaborating Centres, INRB, NICD)
Field laboratories operated by MSF, CDC, EU, and other partners
preventionStrategies: Receive rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo) ring vaccination if identified as a contact or contact-of-contact of a Zaire ebolavirus case.
Pre-exposure vaccination with Ervebo or the two-dose Zabdeno/Mvabea regimen is offered to healthcare workers, laboratory staff, and outbreak responders in or travelling to active outbreak zones.
Avoid contact with bats, non-human primates, and other potentially infected wildlife and do not consume bushmeat in endemic regions.
Use full personal protective equipment when caring for any patient with suspected viral hemorrhagic fever, including impermeable gown, double gloves, N95 respirator or PAPR, and face shield.
Practice safe and dignified burial — bodies of suspected or confirmed cases are handled only by trained teams using sealed body bags and protective equipment.
Male survivors should use condoms or abstain from sex until two consecutive monthly semen RT-PCR tests are negative.
Maintain regular handwashing with soap and water or 0.05% chlorine solution in healthcare and outbreak settings.
lifestyleModifications: Survivors should attend structured one-year follow-up clinics for post-Ebola syndrome screening (joint pain, vision, neurological symptoms, mental health).
Male survivors must use condoms or abstain from sex until semen testing confirms clearance (typically 6-12 months but occasionally longer).
Healthcare workers in endemic regions should remain up to date with PPE training and vaccination.
Travellers returning from outbreak zones should self-monitor temperature for 21 days and seek care immediately for fever.
Community members should engage with public-health teams during outbreaks rather than hide cases, which prolongs transmission and outbreak duration.

recommended: Oral rehydration solution as tolerated during convalescence to support fluid recovery
High-calorie, high-protein foods during recovery to address weight loss and muscle wasting
Iron- and vitamin-rich foods to support recovery from anemia and immune dysfunction
Cooked, safe foods to minimize gastrointestinal complications during recovery
avoid: Bushmeat (bats, primates, antelope, duikers) in endemic regions
Raw or undercooked animal products in outbreak zones
Alcohol during convalescence and while taking medications with hepatic metabolism
Unpasteurized milk products until breast-milk persistence is excluded in lactating survivors

exerciseGuidelines: Gradual return to physical activity is appropriate during convalescence. Many survivors experience fatigue and arthralgia for weeks to months; structured rehabilitation including low-impact aerobic exercise, joint mobility, and graded muscle strengthening accelerates recovery. Avoid contact sports until laboratory follow-up and clinical recovery are complete.
dailyManagement: Strict isolation and PPE protocols within the treatment unit until two consecutive negative RT-PCR tests confirm viral clearance.
Hourly vital signs and fluid balance monitoring during acute illness.
Twice-daily clinical reassessment of dehydration, mental status, bleeding, and organ function.
Family communication facilitated through visitor windows, video calls, or designated communication officers given infection-control constraints.
Detailed exposure history and contact tracing recorded for every confirmed case to inform public-health response.
Survivor care plan documented before discharge — follow-up schedule, semen testing, mental-health support, and vaccination.
prognosis: Outcomes depend on viral strain, time to treatment, viral load at presentation, and quality of supportive care. Historical case-fatality rates ranged from 60-90% for Zaire ebolavirus in resource-limited outbreaks. Modern care with structured fluid resuscitation, monoclonal antibodies, and intensive supportive care has cut mortality to 33-35% for Zaire ebolavirus treated early. Patients with low initial viral load (PCR cycle threshold above 22) have mortality under 20% with either Inmazeb or Ebanga. Sudan and Bundibugyo strains retain higher mortality (40-60%) in the absence of approved species-specific therapy. Survivors face a high burden of post-Ebola syndrome: arthralgia in roughly 75%, ocular symptoms or uveitis in 25-50%, hearing changes, headache, fatigue, depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress, and prolonged amenorrhoea. Persistent virus in semen, breast milk, ocular fluid, and the central nervous system has caused late relapses and onward transmission events years after initial recovery. Most physical symptoms improve over 1-2 years; mental-health sequelae and social stigma can persist longer and need ongoing support. Outbreak duration and total case count have shortened dramatically as vaccine ring strategy and antibody therapy have matured: the 2018-2020 DRC outbreak was contained within 22 months despite armed conflict and the Uganda Sudan outbreak in 2022 was declared over within five months.
recoveryTimeline: Acute illness lasts 6-16 days; survivors typically improve from day 7-10 onwards. Most patients are discharged once two consecutive RT-PCR tests are negative on blood. Convalescence with fatigue, arthralgia, and weight loss lasts 1-3 months in mild cases and up to 12 months in severe cases. Post-Ebola syndrome symptoms (joint pain, eye disease, mental-health symptoms) follow a variable course and require structured 1-year follow-up. Semen RT-PCR is repeated monthly until negative twice consecutively, often 6-12 months and sometimes longer.
complications: Hypovolemic and septic shock with multi-organ failure — leading cause of death; addressed with aggressive supportive care.
Severe coagulopathy with disseminated intravascular coagulation and hemorrhage from multiple sites.
Acute kidney injury requiring renal replacement therapy.
Encephalopathy, seizures, and delayed neurological sequelae from direct CNS involvement.
Persistent Ebola viral RNA in semen, eye, CNS, breast milk producing risk of late transmission and relapse.
Uveitis and other ocular complications (in roughly 25-50% of survivors) that can lead to vision loss if untreated.
Arthralgia, fatigue, and post-traumatic stress disorder in survivors — termed post-Ebola syndrome.
Maternal and fetal death in pregnancy with Ebola — historical maternal mortality near 100% and fetal loss near universal.

supportResources.name: WHO Ebola virus disease — fact sheets and outbreak situation reports
supportResources.description: Global epidemiology, outbreak history, current treatment and vaccine guidance, and live situation reports during outbreaks.

supportResources.name: CDC Ebola (Ebola Virus Disease)
supportResources.description: Clinical, laboratory, and public-health guidance for clinicians, travelers, and the public in the US and globally.

supportResources.name: Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) — Ebola response
supportResources.description: Operational guidance, field reports, and survivor-care insights from MSF, the lead non-governmental responder in many outbreaks.

supportResources.name: Africa CDC Emergency Operations Centre
supportResources.description: African Union public-health body coordinating regional outbreak response and surveillance.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both present with high fever, headache, and myalgia in returning travellers from sub-Saharan Africa. Malaria is diagnosed by blood smear or rapid antigen test and responds to artemisinin therapy. Ebola is confirmed by RT-PCR and treated with monoclonal antibodies and intensive supportive care. Coinfection is common and malaria treatment is given empirically while awaiting Ebola results.

confusedWithConditions.name: Marburg virus disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Marburg virus is a closely related filovirus that causes a clinically indistinguishable viral hemorrhagic fever. Discrimination requires RT-PCR. Marburg outbreaks have occurred in Angola, Uganda, Rwanda, Equatorial Guinea, and Tanzania. No licensed vaccine or antibody therapy exists for Marburg as of 2026.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lassa fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lassa is an arenavirus transmitted predominantly by Mastomys rodents in West Africa. Onset is more gradual than Ebola, hearing loss is a hallmark complication, and ribavirin therapy reduces mortality. RT-PCR distinguishes the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Typhoid fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Typhoid produces a more gradual rising fever with abdominal pain, hepatosplenomegaly, and rose spots; bleeding is rare and confined to gastrointestinal hemorrhage from terminal-ileum ulcers. Diagnosed by blood culture; treated with ceftriaxone or fluoroquinolones.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dengue Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dengue is a flavivirus transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes that can also cause hemorrhagic features, but is geographically broader (Asia, Latin America, Africa) and typically presents with retro-orbital pain, thrombocytopenia, and plasma leakage rather than gastrointestinal Ebola-pattern. Confirmed by NS1 antigen or PCR.

relatedConditions.name: Marburg virus disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Closely related filovirus causing a clinically indistinguishable viral hemorrhagic fever; managed with the same isolation and supportive-care principles.

relatedConditions.name: Lassa fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Another West African viral hemorrhagic fever; differential diagnosis and outbreak response infrastructure overlap.

relatedConditions.name: Yellow Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Vaccine-preventable hemorrhagic flavivirus also endemic in sub-Saharan Africa; differential for fever in returning travellers.

relatedConditions.name: Dengue Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Globally common arboviral hemorrhagic illness; useful differential in returning travellers with fever and bleeding.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: The dominant febrile illness in sub-Saharan Africa and a frequent coinfection; always tested and treated empirically in suspected Ebola.

faqs.question: What is Ebola virus disease?
faqs.answer: Ebola virus disease is a severe and often fatal viral hemorrhagic fever caused by Ebola viruses. It spreads through direct contact with body fluids of infected people or wildlife. Case-fatality rates have ranged from 25 to 90% across outbreaks, with a median around 50%. Approved vaccines and antibody treatments now cut mortality substantially for the Zaire strain.

faqs.question: How does Ebola spread?
faqs.answer: Ebola spreads through direct contact with blood, vomit, stool, urine, sweat, breast milk, semen, and saliva of infected people, or contaminated surfaces. Sexual transmission from male survivors can occur for months. Bats are the suspected reservoir and primates can transmit to humans. It is not airborne.

faqs.question: What are the first symptoms of Ebola?
faqs.answer: Early symptoms appear 2-21 days after exposure (average 8-10 days): sudden fever, severe headache, muscle and joint pain, profound fatigue, and sore throat. By day 3-7 vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain, and a maculopapular rash appear. Hemorrhagic features develop in 30-50% of severe cases.

faqs.question: How long does Ebola incubation last?
faqs.answer: The incubation period is 2 to 21 days, with most cases developing symptoms 8-10 days after exposure. Anyone exposed who remains symptom-free for 21 days is not infected. People are not infectious during incubation; transmission requires symptoms.

faqs.question: Is there a cure for Ebola?
faqs.answer: Two approved monoclonal antibody treatments — Inmazeb (atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab) and Ebanga (ansuvimab) — reduce mortality of Zaire ebolavirus infection from over 50% to under 35% when given early. They are not yet effective against Sudan or Bundibugyo strains. Aggressive supportive care remains the foundation of treatment.

faqs.question: Is there an Ebola vaccine?
faqs.answer: Yes. The rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine (Ervebo) is over 97% effective against Zaire ebolavirus and was licensed in the US and EU in 2019. A two-dose Zabdeno/Mvabea regimen offers durable protection. No species-specific licensed vaccine exists for Sudan or Bundibugyo as of 2026, although candidate vaccines are in trials.

faqs.question: Can you survive Ebola?
faqs.answer: Yes. Survival has improved dramatically with modern care. Patients treated early with monoclonal antibodies and aggressive supportive care have mortality of 25-35% for Zaire ebolavirus and under 20% if the viral load is low at treatment start. Tens of thousands of people have survived Ebola.

faqs.question: What is post-Ebola syndrome?
faqs.answer: Post-Ebola syndrome refers to long-term sequelae in survivors: arthralgia in about 75%, uveitis or other ocular symptoms in 25-50%, fatigue, headache, hearing changes, neurological complications, depression, and anxiety. Most symptoms improve over 1-2 years; some are permanent. Structured 1-year follow-up is standard.

faqs.question: How is Ebola diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by real-time RT-PCR for Ebola viral RNA on blood, performed in a designated biosafety laboratory or with field cartridge platforms (such as GeneXpert Ebola). Antigen ELISA and IgM serology support diagnosis. Sensitivity improves after symptom day 3, and negative tests are repeated in 48-72 hours if suspicion remains high.

faqs.question: Where are Ebola outbreaks happening?
faqs.answer: Ebola outbreaks occur predominantly in equatorial sub-Saharan Africa. The largest recorded outbreak (2014-2016) struck Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone. Major outbreaks have also occurred in DRC, Uganda, Sudan, Republic of Congo, Gabon, Côte d'Ivoire, Mali, and Nigeria. The 2022 Uganda outbreak involved the Sudan strain.

faqs.question: Can Ebola survivors transmit the virus?
faqs.answer: Yes, in specific circumstances. Ebola virus persists in semen, ocular fluid, breast milk, and central nervous system of survivors for months. Sexual transmission and breast-milk transmission have triggered new outbreaks. WHO advises condom use until two consecutive monthly semen RT-PCR tests are negative.

faqs.question: How is Ebola spread different from COVID-19?
faqs.answer: COVID-19 spreads through respiratory droplets and aerosols and is highly transmissible. Ebola requires direct contact with body fluids of a symptomatic patient or recently deceased person and is not airborne. This is why Ebola outbreaks are typically smaller and more geographically limited than respiratory viral outbreaks.

faqs.question: What does Ebola treatment involve?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines monoclonal antibodies for Zaire ebolavirus (Inmazeb or Ebanga), aggressive intravenous fluid and electrolyte resuscitation, empirical antibiotics and antimalarials, symptomatic care (antiemetics, analgesia, oxygen), and organ support including renal replacement when needed. Care is delivered in designated treatment units with full personal protective equipment.

faqs.question: Is Ebola airborne?
faqs.answer: No. Ebola is not transmitted through casual airborne spread under normal patient-care conditions. Aerosolization can occur during certain medical procedures (such as suctioning), and these are managed with airborne-precaution PPE. Outside hospital settings, transmission requires direct contact with body fluids.

faqs.question: What is ring vaccination?
faqs.answer: Ring vaccination is rapid vaccination of all contacts and contacts-of-contacts of a confirmed Ebola case, plus frontline workers, to surround new infections with immune individuals. It uses the rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine and has been the cornerstone of containment since 2015. Ring vaccination has contributed to dramatic shortening of outbreaks.

faqs.question: Can children get Ebola?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children account for roughly 20% of cases in major outbreaks. Outcomes are worst in infants and neonates; older children and adolescents have outcomes similar to young adults. Pediatric care follows the same principles as adult care with weight-based fluid and drug dosing.

faqs.question: Is Ebola contagious before symptoms appear?
faqs.answer: No. People do not transmit Ebola during incubation; transmission requires symptoms and the presence of detectable virus in body fluids. Contacts who remain symptom-free for 21 days after exposure are not infected. Contact tracing is built around this 21-day window.

faqs.question: Can Ebola virus disease come back?
faqs.answer: Late recrudescence has been documented in survivors with persistent virus in immune-privileged sites (eye, central nervous system, semen) and in survivors who develop new symptomatic disease months to years later. The 2021 Guinea outbreak was traced genetically to a survivor of the 2014-2016 epidemic, indicating very long-term viral persistence.

faqs.question: Is Ebola treatment available outside Africa?
faqs.answer: Yes. Suspected cases in non-endemic regions are referred to designated high-consequence infectious disease units (such as CDC-designated regional Ebola treatment centres in the US). Monoclonal antibody therapy and rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine are available through national stockpiles, the WHO emergency reserve, and the International Coordinating Group on Vaccine Provision.

faqs.question: What PPE is needed for Ebola care?
faqs.answer: Healthcare workers in Ebola treatment units wear impermeable gown, double gloves, N95 or PAPR respirator, full face shield, head cover, and waterproof boots. Donning and doffing are performed under supervision to prevent self-contamination. Adherence to PPE protocols cut healthcare-worker mortality dramatically after the 2014-2016 outbreak.

faqs.question: How are Ebola outbreaks contained?
faqs.answer: Outbreak containment combines rapid case identification through RT-PCR, contact tracing of all known exposures, ring vaccination with rVSV-ZEBOV, dedicated treatment units, safe and dignified burial, community engagement and trust-building, infection prevention and control in healthcare, and laboratory surveillance. Most outbreaks are contained within 6-22 months under modern response.
